guide/history.json.title: A Short History of United Arab Emirates
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Prehistoric Settlement
items.summary: Nomadic hunter-gatherers and early pearlers establish settlements along the Arabian Gulf coast.
items.text: Archaeological evidence from sites like Jebel Ali and Umm Al Quwain reveals that the UAE's coastline attracted early human populations seeking fish, pearls, and shellfish. These prehistoric communities lived in small, dispersed groups, adapting to the harsh desert and marine environment. The abundance of natural pearls in the shallow waters would later become the region's most valuable resource, shaping trade networks and settlement patterns for millennia.

items.label: Bronze Age Trade Networks
items.summary: Port settlements emerge as vital trading hubs connecting Mesopotamia, the Indus Valley, and Oman.
items.text: Cities like Umm Al Quwain and Jebel Ali developed into bustling ports, exchanging copper, incense, and pearls with distant civilizations. Archaeological finds of Mesopotamian seals and Harappan pottery demonstrate the region's integration into vast Bronze Age trade networks. These early Emiratis were sophisticated merchants and sailors who understood navigation and commercial exchange across the Arabian Sea.

items.label: Iron Age and Coastal Kingdoms
items.summary: Independent trading states flourish along the coast, controlling valuable pearl and spice routes.
items.text: After the Bronze Age collapse, new city-states emerged with stronger fortifications and more organized political structures. The coastal emirates of the period developed distinct identities while maintaining maritime trade with Persian and Greek merchants. Pearl diving became increasingly sophisticated, with organized fleets and specialized techniques that would define Gulf commerce for centuries.

items.label: Hellenistic Influence
items.summary: Greek culture and commerce penetrate the Gulf following Alexander the Great's eastern campaigns.
items.text: Alexander's conquests brought Greek merchants, coins, and cultural practices to Emirati ports, though direct Greek settlement remained limited. Archaeological evidence of Greek pottery and inscriptions at sites like Mleiha shows cultural contact and trade integration. The period saw increased formalization of trade routes, with Greek merchants seeking pearls and Arabian incense while introducing Mediterranean goods.

items.label: Sassanid Dominance
items.summary: The Persian Sassanid Empire controls the Gulf, extracting taxes and enforcing political hegemony over coastal emirates.
items.text: The rise of the Sassanid Persian Empire brought the emirates under strong external control, with local rulers becoming vassals paying tribute in pearls and taxes. Despite political subjugation, trade flourished, and Sassanid administrative systems influenced local governance structures. The period saw the spread of Zoroastrianism and Christian communities along the coast, with archaeological sites revealing multicultural populations living in major ports.

items.label: Islamic Conquest and Integration
items.summary: Arab Muslim armies rapidly conquer the region, integrating the emirates into the expanding Islamic caliphate.
items.text: The lightning conquest of the Gulf by early Islamic forces transformed the spiritual, political, and cultural landscape of the Emirates. Local populations rapidly embraced Islam, and the region became part of the Umayyad Caliphate's vast domain. This period marked the beginning of Arab cultural dominance and the emergence of Arabic as the universal language, replacing Aramaic and Persian in administrative and commercial contexts.

items.label: Abbasid Period and Pearl Economy
items.summary: Pearl diving becomes the dominant economic activity, enriching local merchants while the emirates remain politically fragmented.
items.text: Under Abbasid rule and its successor dynasties, the emirates developed a sophisticated pearl economy with organized diving seasons, merchant guilds, and trade monopolies. Coastal towns like Dubai, Abu Dhabi, and Sharjah grew wealthy but remained independent city-states under local tribal rulers. The pearl trade created a cosmopolitan merchant culture, attracting traders from Persia, India, and East Africa, yet the region never unified politically, with occasional tribal conflicts disrupting commerce.

items.label: Portuguese Intrusion and Decline
items.summary: Portuguese naval forces invade the Gulf, disrupting pearl trade and establishing regional supremacy.
items.text: Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama's arrival marked the beginning of European interference in Gulf affairs, as the Portuguese navy conquered key ports and imposed taxes on pearl fleets. This period brought economic hardship as Portuguese control disrupted traditional trade routes and pearl diving operations suffered from military conflict. However, Portuguese dominance eventually weakened due to vast logistical challenges and resistance from local rulers and Ottoman allies.

items.label: Omani and European Competition
items.summary: The Sultanate of Oman and European powers contest control of the Gulf while local emirates pursue independence.
items.text: With Portuguese power declining, the Sultanate of Oman emerged as the dominant regional force, though the individual emirates resisted full submission. The period saw increasing English and Dutch commercial interests competing with French and other European traders for Gulf commerce. Local rulers skillfully played rival powers against each other, maintaining nominal independence while benefiting from increased trade competition that stimulated commerce and cultural exchange.

items.label: Wahhabi Wars and British Expansion
items.summary: Religious zealotry and British imperial interests destabilize the region, leading to British military dominance.
items.text: The rise of Wahhabism in central Arabia sparked religious conflicts that reached the UAE coast, while simultaneously British naval forces expanded control over Arabian Gulf shipping lanes. Emirati rulers faced pressure from both fanatical religious movements and British imperial ambitions, struggling to maintain their autonomy. The period culminated in devastating raids on coastal settlements and the rise of piracy, prompting British intervention and the demand for treaties.

items.label: British Protectorate Era
items.summary: Britain imposes formal protectorates over the emirates, suppressing piracy but stifling political independence and economic development.
items.text: The Trucial States agreement of 1853 marked the beginning of formal British protectorate rule, with Britain controlling foreign policy and defense while local sheikhs retained internal authority. This arrangement brought stability and ended piracy, but froze the emirates in economic stagnation as traditional pearl diving collapsed due to Japanese cultured pearls and global economic depression. For over a century, the emirates remained sleepy backwater sheikhdoms, their rulers impoverished and their populations with limited opportunities, sustained only by subsistence pearling and small-scale trade.

items.label: Oil Discovery and Transformation
items.summary: Oil discoveries revolutionize the emirates' fortunes, promising unprecedented wealth and forcing preparation for independence.
items.text: Abu Dhabi's first major oil strike in 1960 initiated the transformation from poverty to phenomenal wealth, fundamentally altering the region's trajectory. The sudden influx of oil revenues enabled massive infrastructure development, education investments, and population influx as workers arrived from across the world. The discovery of oil also accelerated the path to independence as Britain began withdrawing from imperial commitments, with local rulers recognizing the need for unified governance to maximize their new power.

items.label: Modern UAE Formation and Development
items.summary: Seven emirates unite as an independent nation, leveraging oil wealth to build a global economic powerhouse and iconic modern state.
items.text: On December 2, 1971, the United Arab Emirates was officially born when six emirates united under the federal constitution, joined by Ras al-Khaimah in 1972. Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi became the federation's first president and guiding visionary, directing massive oil revenues into education, healthcare, and infrastructure while maintaining traditional values. The UAE rapidly transformed from desert poverty to one of the world's most developed nations, with Dubai pioneering tourism and finance, establishing free trade zones, and building iconic structures like the Burj Khalifa. Today the UAE stands as a beacon of Arab modernity, balancing rapid globalization with Islamic tradition while diversifying its economy beyond oil into finance, technology, and tourism.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in United Arab Emirates — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Arabic Coffee Ceremony
items.text: Qahwa is served in small handleless cups and is traditionally black, cardamom-scented, and strong. Accept at least one cup when offered in homes or majlis settings—it's a sign of hospitality and refusing is considered rude. The server will pour from a long-spouted dallah (pot) and you signal you're finished by tilting and returning the cup.

items.label: Al Reef Bakery
items.text: A local chain serving traditional Emirati breakfast with fresh karak tea and luqaimat (fried dough balls). Arrive early as the best items sell out by mid-morning and prices are refreshingly low.

items.label: Karak Tea Over Coffee
items.text: Despite the qahwa tradition, many Emiratis prefer spiced karak tea (black tea with cardamom, cinnamon, and evaporated milk). You'll find it everywhere from street vendors to cafés and it's cheaper and more accessible than specialty coffee.

items.label: Ripe Market Coffee Stands
items.text: The farmers' markets in Dubai and Abu Dhabi have excellent local coffee vendors serving fresh Arabic coffee alongside international options. The atmosphere is authentically local and prices are fair.

items.label: Avoid Obvious Tourist Traps
items.text: Skip the beachfront restaurants in Jumeirah and Marinas unless you want to pay 3x normal prices for mediocre food. The best meals are found in older neighborhoods like Deira, Al Fahidi, and Karama in Dubai, or Al Mina in Abu Dhabi.

items.label: Al Mallah (Lebanese)
items.text: A legendary shawarma and grilled meat spot in Deira that's been packed for decades with locals and tourists alike. Order the chicken shawarma, arrive off-peak to avoid 30-minute waits, and expect to eat standing at the counter or outside.

items.label: Zaroob (Emirati Fast Casual)
items.text: Modern take on traditional Emirati street food with branches across the UAE—try the camel meat machboos or madrouba. It's affordable, quick, and gives you authentic flavors without formal dining pretense.

items.label: Friday Brunch Culture
items.text: Every decent hotel and many restaurants offer all-you-can-eat Friday brunches (usually 12-4pm) with unlimited food, drinks, and sometimes alcohol. Book ahead as popular spots fill up; expect to spend 200-400 AED per person but it's often better value than dining à la carte.

items.label: Ramadan Dining Changes
items.text: During Ramadan, many restaurants close during daylight hours, alcohol is not served, and dining customs shift dramatically. Eating or drinking in public during fasting hours is illegal and disrespectful—plan accordingly and enjoy the evening iftar buffets instead.

items.label: Pakistani & Indian Areas (Karama, Bur Dubai)
items.text: These neighborhoods offer the cheapest and often most flavorful meals in the Emirates—excellent biryani, karahi, and dosa from family-run shops. Skip the fancy malls and eat where Emirati blue-collar workers eat.

items.label: Machboos (Emirati Rice)
items.text: Spiced rice cooked with meat (traditionally camel, now often chicken or lamb) in one pot, finished with crispy onions and served with yogurt. It's the national dish and appears at every formal gathering—try it at local restaurants rather than hotels for authentic preparation.

items.label: Shawarma
items.text: Thin-sliced marinated meat (chicken or lamb) carved from a rotating spit, wrapped in flatbread with tahini, tomato, and onion. It's the street food of choice and shouldn't cost more than 8-12 AED; Al Mallah in Deira serves the most famous version.

items.label: Luqaimat
items.text: Fried dough balls drizzled with date syrup and dusted with sesame seeds, traditionally eaten at breakfast or during Ramadan. They're crispy outside, soft inside, and addictive—buy them fresh from bakeries rather than pre-made versions in supermarkets.

items.label: Harees
items.text: A slow-cooked stew of meat, wheat, and clarified butter that's creamy and comfort-food-like, traditionally served during Ramadan and special occasions. It requires hours of preparation so it's rare in restaurants—seek it out at Emirati home-style eateries.

items.label: Tabbouleh & Hummus (Lebanese)
items.text: While not exclusively Emirati, Lebanese mezze culture is everywhere in the UAE and these are essential starters. The parsley salad should be bright and lemony, the hummus should be silky—avoid versions that taste mass-produced or stale.

items.label: Fish from the Gulf
items.text: Hammour (grouper) and other local white fish are best enjoyed grilled simply with lemon and served at seafood-focused restaurants. Avoid overly complicated sauces that mask the delicate flesh; waterfront restaurants in fishing villages like Ajman or Umm Al Quwain offer the freshest catches at fair prices.

items.label: Menu Language Expectations
items.text: Most menus in Dubai and Abu Dhabi are in both Arabic and English, but smaller towns may have Arabic-only menus. Download Google Translate's camera feature or ask staff for help—they're used to language barriers and will guide you honestly.

items.label: Mezze vs. Mains
items.text: Middle Eastern restaurants structure meals differently from Western ones. Small plates (mezze) come first and are meant for sharing—order several rather than one large main. Bread, rice, and salads arrive separately and are included, not extras.

items.label: Grilled Meat Section
items.text: Look for dishes labeled 'mishwe' (grilled), 'kabab', or 'shish'—these are usually the most reliable items. Meat quality and flavor varies dramatically by restaurant, so ask the server what's freshest or most popular that day.

items.label: Avoid Overly Tourist-Oriented Translations
items.text: If you see cutesy English translations like 'dancing chicken' or flowery descriptions everywhere, it's likely a tourist trap. Authentic local restaurants use straightforward names and let the food speak for itself.

items.label: Bread is Free
items.text: Complimentary bread (usually flatbread like khubz) arrives with every meal in Middle Eastern restaurants. You're not charged for it—it's part of the dining experience and meant to accompany every bite of food or sauce.

items.label: Alcohol Laws Are Strict
items.text: Alcohol is only served in hotels, licensed restaurants, and bars—never in street-food venues or homes. Public intoxication is illegal and can result in fines or deportation; drinking non-Muslims into intoxication is particularly looked down upon.

items.label: Wine & Spirits Pricing
items.text: Alcohol in the UAE is heavily taxed and expensive compared to Western countries—expect to pay 50+ AED for wine and 12+ AED for beer. Hotel restaurants and standalone bars charge premium prices; some villa compounds and beach clubs offer better value.

items.label: Fresh Juices & Smoothies
items.text: Freshly squeezed orange, mango, and pomegranate juices are ubiquitous, cheap (3-5 AED), and delicious—better than any bottled option. Juice stands are found everywhere from markets to mall food courts.

items.label: Arabic Tea & Mint Lemonade
items.text: Spiced black tea served with fresh mint is a refreshing alternative to coffee and available everywhere, especially in afternoons. Mint lemonade is also common, particularly in summer, and both are alcohol-free traditional choices.

items.label: Water & Hydration
items.text: Tap water is safe to drink throughout the UAE and is actually desalinated seawater—perfectly drinkable. However, bottled water is cheap and ubiquitous; always carry water in the intense heat and drink constantly to avoid dehydration.

items.label: Right Hand for Eating
items.text: In traditional settings and when eating with hands (bread, shawarma, rice), always use your right hand—the left is considered unclean in Islamic culture. If you're left-handed, do your best; locals understand it may be difficult but will appreciate the effort.

items.label: Remove Shoes in Homes & Majlis
items.text: If invited to an Emirati home or traditional majlis for a meal, remove your shoes at the entrance. Sit on the cushions provided and wait to be invited to eat—the host serves and you should eat slowly and socially, not rush.

items.label: Compliment the Host
items.text: Saying 'bismillah' (in the name of God) before eating and complimenting the food sincerely is expected and appreciated. Never refuse food offered by a host—take at least a small portion and eat it respectfully.

items.label: Gender Separation in Traditional Settings
items.text: In conservative Emirati homes or majlis, men and women may eat separately or sit in different areas. Follow the host's lead without comment—this is normal and respectful, not an insult.

items.label: Tipping & Bills
items.text: In restaurants, 10-15% tip is customary and expected in upscale dining but optional in casual spots. Many bills already include a service charge (check before adding tip). Round up or leave small change at casual eateries.

items.label: Dining Timing & Hours
items.text: Emiratis eat dinner very late (9-11pm) compared to Western schedules; many restaurants don't get busy until 9pm. Lunch is the main meal (1-3pm) and everything closes or slows during prayer times—plan accordingly.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around United Arab Emirates.

items.label: Etihad Rail Coverage
items.text: Etihad Rail currently operates limited passenger service between select stations in Abu Dhabi and Al Ain, with plans for major expansion to Dubai and other emirates by 2030. The journey from Abu Dhabi to Al Ain takes about 2 hours and offers a scenic route through the desert.

items.label: Booking and Stations
items.text: Purchase tickets online via the Etihad Rail website or mobile app, or at station ticket offices. Major stations include Abu Dhabi Central Station and Al Ain Station, both located conveniently near city centers.
Service frequency is limited with only a few trains daily on existing routes, so plan accordingly and book in advance during peak travel periods.

items.label: Dubai Metro and Bus Network
items.text: The Dubai Metro (Red and Green lines) is the fastest way to navigate Dubai, running from 5:00 AM to midnight (2:00 AM on weekends). Buses cover the entire emirate with hundreds of routes operated by the Roads and Transport Authority (RTA).

items.label: Nol Card Payment
items.text: Use a Nol card (reloadable smart card) for all metro and bus journeys in Dubai—it's faster than buying individual tickets and offers slightly lower fares. Cards are available at metro stations and convenience stores across the city.

items.label: Abu Dhabi and Other Emirates
items.text: Abu Dhabi has an extensive bus network (Etihad Mobility) and no metro. Sharjah and other emirates also rely on buses; consider using the national transport app or local apps for route planning.
Buses can be crowded during rush hours (7–9 AM and 4–7 PM) and air conditioning varies; women-only sections are available on select services.
Fines for fare evasion on metro and buses range from AED 100 to AED 500; inspectors regularly check tickets and Nol card validity.

items.label: Official Taxi Services
items.text: Hail white-and-black taxis operated by Dubai Taxi Corporation or use their official app to book with meter rates. Abu Dhabi has its own taxi fleet (white vehicles) with similar metered services.

items.label: Uber and Careem
items.text: Uber and Careem (owned by Uber) operate widely across UAE; fares are higher than traditional taxis but rides are tracked and payment is cashless. Both apps work reliably in Dubai, Abu Dhabi, and other major cities.

items.label: Booking and Payment
items.text: Book taxis via phone, hailing, or apps; most official taxis accept both cash and card, while ride-sharing apps require a credit or debit card linked to your account.
Unlicensed taxis and unofficial drivers pose safety and scam risks—always use official white taxis or verified app-based services to avoid inflated fares and safety concerns.
Surge pricing on Uber and Careem during peak hours (evenings, weekends, events) can double or triple fares; plan travel timing accordingly.

items.label: International Driving Permit & Requirements
items.text: Visitors can drive using a valid international driving permit (IDP) and passport for up to 30 days; ensure your home country license is accompanied by an official English translation or IDP.

items.label: Car Rental Services
items.text: Major international car rental companies (Hertz, Budget, Europcar) and local firms operate at airports and city centers. Rates are competitive, and insurance (basic comprehensive coverage recommended) is mandatory.
Speed limits are strictly enforced via radar cameras; fines for speeding are severe (AED 300–3,000+) and violations are recorded in the system, affecting future fines and insurance.
Seatbelts are mandatory for all passengers; using your phone while driving, drink-driving, and reckless driving incur heavy fines (AED 500–3,000) and potential vehicle impoundment or jail time.
Desert driving and off-road routes require experience and proper permits; sandstorms and extreme heat can strand vehicles, so inform someone of your route and carry water and a charged phone.

items.label: Salik Toll System
items.text: The Salik electronic toll system applies to many highways in Dubai and Abu Dhabi; rental cars usually have tags, and charges appear on your rental bill or via your Salik account.

items.label: Major Airports
items.text: Dubai International (DXB) is the world's busiest airport by international passenger traffic. Abu Dhabi International (AUH) is the main hub for Etihad Airways and serves the capital region efficiently.

items.label: Ground Transport from Airports
items.text: Dubai Airport Express buses connect DXB to city center (routes 401 and 402; ~1–2 hours, AED 5–20 via Nol card). Taxis and Uber/Careem are available 24/7; trains from DXB to downtown are planned for 2024 launch.

items.label: Visa & Immigration
items.text: UAE offers on-arrival visas for most nationalities (30–180 days); processing is usually fast at immigration. Use e-gates if your passport is biometric-chip enabled for quicker clearance.

Prohibited items include drugs, alcohol (unless duty-free), certain medications, and electronic devices requiring specific permits; penalties for contraband are severe (fines up to AED 100,000 or imprisonment).

items.label: Airport Connectivity & Services
items.text: Free WiFi is available throughout both DXB and AUH; ATMs and money changers are abundant at competitive rates; SIM cards from major providers are sold at kiosks.

items.label: UAE Ferry Routes
items.text: Ferry services operate between Dubai and Musandam (Oman), and within the Persian Gulf; operators include the National Ferries Company and private ferry providers.

items.label: Booking & Fares
items.text: Book ferries online or at ticket offices in city centers (e.g., Dubai Creek Harbour, Abu Dhabi Corniche); fares are reasonable (AED 50–200 for passenger journeys) and trips typically take 1–3 hours.

items.label: Scenic Water Taxis
items.text: Water taxis and abra (traditional wooden boat) services operate in Dubai Creek and Abu Dhabi, offering an affordable (AED 1–5) and scenic alternative to roads for short distances.
Ferry schedules can be irregular, especially during high winds or rough seas; confirm departure times in advance and arrive early during peak seasons.

items.label: Nol Card & Daily Passes
items.text: The Nol card (Silver for regular use, Gold for frequent travelers) offers the best value in Dubai for unlimited metro and bus travel; daily passes (AED 15–25) are available for tourists.

items.label: Multi-Day Tourist Passes
items.text: Tourist packages (24-hour, 48-hour, 72-hour passes) provide unlimited metro, bus, and some tram journeys in Dubai starting from AED 25; purchase at stations or via the RTA app.

items.label: Inter-Emirate Travel
items.text: Buses connecting Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, and other emirates operate from main bus stations; fares are fixed (AED 10–50 depending on distance) and services run hourly or more frequently on major routes.
Always carry proof of payment (Nol card, ticket receipt, or app confirmation) on public transport; inspectors conduct random checks and issue fines (AED 100–500) for non-compliance.
Discounted passes are often sold by unofficial vendors at inflated prices; purchase only from official RTA ticket offices and machines to avoid scams or invalid cards.

items.label: Major Telecom Providers
items.text: Etisalat and Ooredoo are the main mobile operators in UAE, with excellent 4G/5G coverage nationwide. Both offer prepaid and postpaid plans with data, calls, and SMS bundles.

items.label: Buying a SIM Card
items.text: Purchase SIM cards at airports, telecom shops, malls, and convenience stores without lengthy registration; prepaid plans start as low as AED 10–50 with data and minutes included.

items.label: Data & Roaming Plans
items.text: Local SIM cards offer excellent value for data (unlimited plans from AED 50–100/month); international roaming is expensive, so a local SIM is recommended for extended stays.
VPNs and proxy services may be restricted or monitored by authorities; downloading copyrighted content or accessing banned websites (gambling, adult content) can result in fines or legal issues.

items.label: Tourist WiFi & Apps
items.text: Free public WiFi is widely available at malls, cafes, airports, and metro stations; apps like Google Maps, Uber, and RTA's official apps work seamlessly and are essential for navigation.
